data.title: Is Barrick Gold Still Worth a Look After 21.8% Surge and Strong Cash Flow Growth?
data.description:
Thinking about what to do with your Barrick Mining shares or considering jumping in? You are not alone. The stock has been on a wild ride lately, and investors are taking notice. With a one-year return of 80.7% and an impressive 111.6% gain year to date, Barrick has outperformed many of its peers. Even in the past 30 days, shares have surged 21.8%, showing renewed enthusiasm after a stretch of relative quiet in the mining sector.

Behind these moves is a mix of market forces, including everything from global commodity price swings to shifts in investor risk appetite. A particularly notable factor has been renewed demand for gold and copper, both driving Barrick’s top-line expectations. The stock’s three-year growth of 161.9% highlights that this appears to be more than just a short-term surge, but genuine momentum building over the longer term.

But is the company really undervalued after this run? To answer that, I have dug into a range of valuation checks, and Barrick scores a 4 out of 6, which definitely stands out among major resource companies. Next, let’s look at exactly how that score came together, walk through each valuation approach in turn, and consider whether there might be an even better way to understand what Barrick is truly worth.

Why Barrick Mining is lagging behind its peers

Advertisement

Approach 1: Barrick Mining Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model takes the company’s expected future cash flows and projects them forward, then discounts those amounts back to today’s value using a required rate of return. This lets investors estimate how much Barrick Mining is really worth, based on its future ability to generate real cash for shareholders.

Barrick Mining’s latest reported Free Cash Flow sits at $1.43 Billion, with analysts projecting steady growth over the next decade. In 2029, estimates call for Free Cash Flow to rise to $3.36 Billion as demand for gold and copper remains robust. Projections for the years beyond 2029 are based on extrapolations by Simply Wall St, and highlight the potential for continued increases, albeit at a more moderate pace as the business matures.

This DCF analysis arrives at a fair value of $52.09 per share for Barrick Mining. With the current share price sitting about 6.5% below this estimated value, the stock is judged to be ABOUT RIGHT in terms of valuation at this time. It is not a significant bargain, but not overhyped either.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Barrick Mining.

Simply Wall St performs a valuation analysis on every stock in the world every day (check out Barrick Mining's valuation analysis). We show the entire calculation in full. You can track the result in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted when this changes.

Approach 2: Barrick Mining Price vs Earnings (PE)

The Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is a classic valuation tool for profitable companies like Barrick Mining because it directly connects the company’s share price to its reported earnings. Investors use the PE ratio as a simple, intuitive snapshot of how much they are paying for each dollar of profit today. For businesses with steady revenue, positive earnings, and some growth potential, the PE ratio is widely accepted for evaluating whether a stock is expensive or reasonably valued.

However, what counts as a “normal” or “fair” PE ratio can change depending on factors such as earnings growth and business risk. Faster-growing companies or those with stable and predictable earnings generally trade at higher PE multiples compared to slower-growing or riskier firms. Riskier businesses or those facing uncertainty often see their PE discounted by the market.

Barrick Mining currently trades at 21.4 times earnings. For context, the average PE among Metals and Mining industry peers is 23.7 times, and the broader peer group average stands at 41.2 times. Simply Wall St goes a step further with the “Fair Ratio,” which is a proprietary estimate that factors in Barrick's own profit margins, earnings growth outlook, industry dynamics, and market cap. Instead of simply comparing to industry or peers, the Fair Ratio (in this case, 25.9 times) is tailored to Barrick’s specific profile and may serve as a more accurate benchmark of value. With Barrick’s actual PE at 21.4 times and the Fair Ratio at 25.9 times, this analysis suggests the stock is priced about right for its expected performance and risks.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

data.title: Is Open Text’s 39% Rally in 2025 Just the Beginning?
data.description:
Thinking about what to do with your Open Text shares, or maybe considering a new investment? You are definitely not alone. Open Text stock has not just recovered from its past slumps, it is making headlines with a 38.6% gain so far this year and a solid 19.3% return over the past twelve months. Just this past week, the share price moved higher by 1.3%. If you look back over the last three years, the stock is up an impressive 58%. That is the kind of performance that attracts attention, especially as tech market optimism has returned across software and cloud companies.

Of course, price moves tell only part of the story. What is really getting attention right now is Open Text’s valuation. It is currently undervalued in five out of six key fundamental checks, earning the company a strong value score of 5. That stands out in a tech environment where many growth names appear expensive and riskier. But is all that upside already priced in, or is there more room for growth? Let’s examine how Open Text measures up across the major valuation methods, and then explore an alternative way to look at valuation that could help inform your decision.

Why Open Text is lagging behind its peers

Advertisement

Approach 1: Open Text Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates the fair value of a company by projecting its future free cash flows and discounting them back to today, reflecting the time value of money. This method helps assess whether the current market price reflects the company’s actual cash-generating potential.

For Open Text, the latest reported Free Cash Flow is $687.7 Million. Analysts provide projections for the next several years, with Free Cash Flow expected to reach $1.03 Billion by 2027. Longer-term forecasts, extrapolated from analyst trends, suggest Free Cash Flow could reach almost $1.5 Billion by 2035. All cash flows are evaluated in U.S. Dollars.

Using these cash flow estimates, the DCF model arrives at an intrinsic value of $66.64 per share. With the current share price trading at a 41.3% discount to this estimated fair value, the DCF approach strongly suggests that Open Text stock is significantly undervalued at the moment.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Open Text.

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Open Text is undervalued by 41.3%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover more undervalued stocks.

Approach 2: Open Text Price vs Earnings (PE)

The Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is especially useful for valuing established, profitable companies like Open Text, as it relates the stock price to actual reported earnings. Investors often look to the PE ratio to gauge how much they are paying for each dollar of earnings, making it a practical tool for companies with steady profits.

Market expectations for growth and risk play a key role in shaping what is considered a “normal” or “fair” PE ratio. Fast-growing, lower-risk companies tend to justify higher PE ratios, while companies facing growth or operational challenges might trade at lower multiples. That is why it is important to look beyond the headline number and understand the wider context.

Open Text currently trades on a PE ratio of 22.4x, which is much lower than the average for its software industry peers at 39.5x and the broader industry average of 34.9x. Peer group comparisons can be helpful, but they do not always account for the unique characteristics of each company.

This is where Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio comes in. The Fair Ratio, 36.5x in this case, incorporates company-specific factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, risk profile, industry classification, and market cap. Unlike blanket peer or industry averages, the Fair Ratio is designed to reflect a more personalized, data-driven context for Open Text’s valuation.

Comparing Open Text’s actual PE of 22.4x to its Fair Ratio of 36.5x, the shares appear to be significantly undervalued on this basis, supporting the findings from the DCF approach.

Result: UNDERVALUED

PE ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Discover companies where insiders are betting big on explosive growth.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Open Text Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives. A Narrative is your own investment story about a company, where you connect your outlook, such as your expectations for revenue, profit margins, and other key metrics, to a financial forecast and, ultimately, to a fair value. Narratives make it simple to combine what you know about a company, like Open Text’s shift to the cloud or AI, with the numbers behind the scenes, helping you decide when a stock looks attractive or too risky.

On Simply Wall St's Community page, millions of investors use Narratives to express their perspectives and see how Fair Value changes as new information comes in. Unlike static models, Narratives update automatically when fresh news, earnings results, or guidance is released, keeping your view current and actionable.

For example, some investors’ Narratives for Open Text see long-term benefits in its global AI integration and assign a higher fair value, using the latest data, one bull projects a price as high as $48.00. Others take a more cautious view based on leadership changes or cloud risks and arrive at a much lower fair value, down to $31.00. This diversity of perspectives empowers you to analyze, compare, and act on the story that matches your own expectations.
